g.data-narration: Welcome to Color Smoothing and Sharpening - essential techniques for enhancing color images while preserving color relationships.
Unlike grayscale images with one channel, color images have multiple channels that must be processed together to preserve relationships.
Different color spaces serve different purposes: RGB for display, HSI for intuitive color editing, YUV for video compression.
Component-wise smoothing applies grayscale filters independently to each RGB channel - simple but may cause color bleeding.
The averaging filter computes the mean of all pixel values in a 3×3 neighborhood for each color channel separately.
Vector median filtering treats RGB as a 3D vector and selects the pixel minimizing total distance to neighbors - better edge preservation.
g.text: Process each pixel as color vector v = [R, G, B]ᵀ
2. Compute sum of distances to all vectors

g.data-narration: In HSI space, smooth only the Intensity channel to preserve Hue and Saturation. In YUV, smooth Y less aggressively than U/V.

g.text: RGB → Convert to HSI → Smooth I only → Convert back
RGB → Convert → Smooth Y aggressively, U/V mildly

g.data-narration: Each smoothing method has trade-offs: component-wise is fast but may artifact, vector median preserves edges but is computationally heavy.
Color sharpening enhances edges by applying high-pass filters to each RGB channel - may create color halos at edges.
Unsharp masking formula: Sharpened = Original + k × (Original - Blurred), where k controls enhancement strength.
Luminance-based sharpening processes only the intensity channel in HSI/YUV - avoids color artifacts by not modifying hue directly.
Watch for color bleeding, halo artifacts, saturation loss, and gamut violations - know how to identify and prevent each.
Remember: Choose component-wise for simplicity, vector methods for quality, luminance-based to prevent color artifacts.
